scenario.description: A regional gas utility planning engineer and a city public works infrastructure planner must jointly determine how to reroute medium-pressure gas mains to accommodate a new municipal transit corridor while maintaining supply reliability and staying within the city’s construction constraints. Each party controls different datasets, design standards, and approval levers, so the rerouting plan cannot be finalized without integrating both operational and urban planning perspectives.

goal.content: Produce a jointly approved network layout document that specifies the agreed rerouting configuration of the affected gas mains along the new transit corridor.
requirements: Include a corridor overview section that lists: (a) the geographic limits of the new transit corridor (start and end intersections), (b) each street segment impacted (by street name and block range), (c) the existing medium-pressure gas mains within or adjacent to the corridor (pipe diameter, material, operating pressure, installation year), and (d) a concise statement of why rerouting is required for each segment.

Include a rerouting configuration table that, for every affected existing main segment, specifies: (a) unique segment ID, (b) current alignment description (street, side, offset from curb, depth), (c) proposed new alignment description, (d) proposed pipe size and material, (e) design operating pressure, (f) length of new main, and (g) status of the existing main (abandon in place, remove, partial removal).

Include a hydraulic and capacity summary that lists for the rerouted network: (a) each affected pressure zone, (b) peak-hour flow assumptions used, (c) modeled minimum and maximum pressures at each critical node (feeder junctions, district regulators, large-load customers) before and after rerouting, and (d) any required capacity upgrades (e.g., upsizing specific segments or adding a looped main), each tied to specific pipe segment IDs in the configuration table.

Include a corridor coordination and constraints matrix that, for each corridor segment, specifies: (a) applicable city utility placement standard cross-section (naming the standard drawing or section), (b) required horizontal and vertical clearances to transit structures, water, sewer, and other major utilities, (c) right-of-way or easement requirements for the rerouted main (new easement, use of existing ROW, or franchise area), and (d) construction restrictions such as allowable work hours, seasonal moratoria, and required coordination with other scheduled municipal projects.

Include an implementation phasing and approvals section that: (a) breaks the rerouting into named phases or work packages with defined geographic limits, (b) lists for each phase the required system outages or tie-in windows and maximum allowable outage duration, (c) identifies for each phase the responsible lead organization (utility or city), and (d) provides a joint sign-off table with dated approval blocks for the MetroGas planning engineer and the City of Riverton infrastructure planner, explicitly referencing the document version and plan sheets approved.

profile.organization: MetroGas Distribution Utility, Network Planning Department
profile.expertise: Medium- and low-pressure gas network planning, including hydraulic modeling, system capacity analysis, and risk-based asset relocation strategies, with authority over technical feasibility and system reliability requirements for pipeline rerouting.

privacy_policy.content: Exact GIS segment identifiers and node identifiers must be removed or replaced with anonymized labels before sharing technical network data with external parties.
privacy_policy.reason: The GIS extract and hydraulic model export expose internal asset and node IDs (e.g., MG-MP-07132 and N-4401) that uniquely identify specific pieces of infrastructure, so they must be anonymized before being shared.

profile.organization: City of Riverton, Department of Public Works and Infrastructure Planning
profile.expertise: Urban infrastructure corridor planning, right-of-way management, and construction phasing, with decision rights on street alignments, utility placement standards, and coordination with other municipal projects impacting the gas network.

privacy_policy.content: Internal standard drawing identifiers and phase identifiers must be generalized or mapped to descriptive names before sharing planning information with external parties.
privacy_policy.reason: The utility cross-section standards and corridor phase definitions contain internal reference IDs (e.g., CS-UT-12 and PH-1) that directly expose proprietary coding schemes, so these identifiers should be replaced with non-coded descriptions before sharing.
